name: Don Pullen
abstract: Don Gabriel Pullen (December 25, 1941 – April 22, 1995) was an American jazz pianist and organist. Pullen developed a strikingly individual style throughout his career.

concerts.label: The Charles Mingus Quintet featuring Dizzy Gillespie, Trumpet
An Evening with Charles Mingus and Old Friends
Tribute to Charles Mingus: Mingus Music

about_quotes.speaker: Jane Bunnett
about_quotes.evidence: Dewey Redman and Don Pullen they were very big mentors to me and they were probably two of the heaviest musicians sort of out there on the scene. And they were so encouraging to me.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jane Bunnett interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, January 25, 2024

about_quotes.speaker: Carmen Lundy
about_quotes.evidence: And I can tell you it was Roland Prince on guitar and Don Pullen on organ, Bucky Thorpe on flugelhorn and Bobby Battle from Detroit on drums. And I did that for a year.
about_quotes.doc_title: Carmen Lundy interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, February 15, 2023

about_quotes.speaker: Chico Freeman
about_quotes.evidence: Actually the original band was great, it originally was Don Pullen on piano, we had Buster Williams on bass and we had Idris Muhammad in the rhythm section.
about_quotes.doc_title: Chico Freeman interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 13, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Alvin Queen
about_quotes.evidence: And I went out on the road with Earl Swanson and the organ player was Don Pullen.
about_quotes.doc_title: Alvin Queen interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, August 23, 1997 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Jack DeJohnette
about_quotes.evidence: Don came from St. Louis, because Don stayed at my house for a week... Don Pullen was a friend of a bass player named Lenny... Don came through Chicago and this is before he started playing free and he stayed at my house on his way to New York.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jack DeJohnette — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Sheila Jordan
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee (Sheila Jordan) does not mention Don Pullen. The interviewer mentions him in the context of Cameron Brown's group, but Sheila Jordan provides no personal interaction or relationship details regarding him.
about_quotes.doc_title: Sheila Jordan — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
